text: Hi this is Dr. Todd Harris from California Hernia Specialists. A lot of times when patients come into our office they want to hear about how we do hernia repair and in particular often laparoscopic hernia repair. I think open hernia repair is a lot more common and patients can understand that a little bit better but when we do laparoscopic hernia repair patients don't always understand exactly how we do that surgery. So we try to explain to them in the office, but often it's difficult. So we wanted to record one of our surgeries and show it to you here today. So today's surgery, you're going to see a laparoscopic inguinal hernia repair. During the surgery, we put a balloon in underneath the muscles, we pull the intestines down and out of the hernia, and we put in a small mesh to repair the space. After we're done the hernia repair, we sew the muscle back up on the outside where we put the camera, and the patient's discharged home an hour or two after surgery. We hope that you enjoyed this video and learned from the case today. Certainly with any questions in the future, we encourage you to call our office or email us. Thank you.

The surgeon inserts a laparoscopic camera through the umbilical region, inflates a balloon beneath the muscle to push the intestines down, and then removes the balloon to expose the raw muscle. An anesthetic is applied to the muscle, followed by the insertion of trocars as operating ports. Two instruments are introduced to locate the intestine protruding through the muscle defect, with visible manipulation of surrounding fat tissue.

The surgeon identifies the internal ring and the inferior epigastric blood vessels, noting the space indicative of a direct hernia. The lining around the intestines is visible, tracking behind the blood vessels. The surgeon carefully pulls the intestinal lining away from the cord structures, which include arteries and veins leading to the testicle, ensuring the lining is completely removed from the hernia site and pulled down to prevent recurrence.

The surgeon uses instruments to pull fat off the pubic tubercle, exposing the bone in the pelvis to prepare for mesh insertion. No hernia is visible in the direct space. The mesh is rolled up, inserted through the camera port, and then unrolled in the cleared space.

The surgeon rotates the mesh into position to cover the hernia defect where fat and intestine had previously protruded. Purple dissolvable tacks are placed above the bone to secure the mesh, ensuring it remains in place during healing. Additional tacks are applied around the edges and near the hernia site to prevent recurrence. The gas is reduced, allowing the intestines and their lining to settle back into their normal position, covering the mesh.

We hope that you enjoyed the video and that you were able to learn a little bit about what we do here at California Hernia Specialists. In the future, if you have any questions or concerns, please feel free to give our office a call or you can email us. We're always here to help and we're always here to answer your questions both before and after your surgery. Thanks again and we hope to see you soon.
